title: Wet-felted needle roll
subtitle: A slim roll-up case for sewing and felting needles
excerpt: Wet felt a long narrow panel, fold it around a rolled section, and secure with a length of cord to make a compact needle roll. The case holds up to twenty needles and rolls closed for storage or travel.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from traditional wool needle-case practice. Felted needle rolls and needle books are a well-established beginner project in UK wet-felting workshops.

glossaryTerms.definition: Wool fibre in a long continuous ribbon, with the fibres lying roughly parallel. Roving is the standard starting form for wet felting flat panels.
The final working stage of wet felting when the panel is rubbed, rolled, or thrown to shrink it and consolidate the fibres into a dense, stable cloth.

content.text: A needle roll is a long felt rectangle, about 25 cm by 12 cm when finished, that folds into a compact roll for storage. One-third of the length is kept as a flat flap; the remaining two-thirds is folded back on itself to form a pocket. Sewing needles are pressed through the single-layer flap; felting needles and longer pins go in the pocket.
Place the felting net on the bamboo mat. Lay out

in a rectangle about 35 cm by 18 cm. Use four layers in alternating directions. Plan for the finished panel to be about 25 cm by 12 cm after shrinkage. Optionally, add a stripe of a contrasting colour across the width at the fold line.

Drizzle warm water over the panel slowly until all layers are saturated. Rub a little soap across the net surface. Rub the whole panel through the net with flat, circular pressure for 8 to 10 minutes. Lift the net, flip the panel, replace the net, and rub the other side for a further 5 minutes.

by rolling the panel up in the bamboo mat and rolling back and forth firmly for 3 minutes. Unroll, turn the panel 90 degrees, and roll again. Repeat until the panel measures approximately 25 cm by 12 cm. Rinse in cool water and press out excess moisture in a towel. Dry flat.

When dry, fold the lower third of the panel up to create a pocket and stitch the two side seams with a few overhand stitches in wool thread. Stitch the ribbon or cord to the centre of one long edge so it wraps around the roll when closed. Press needles through the upper flap and roll up from the open end.

items.problem: The panel is too thick and stiff for needles to push through
items.cause: Too many roving layers were used, or the felting was continued too long
items.fix: Aim for three layers rather than four on the flap section next time. A well-made flat panel should be around 3 mm thick when dry, which most sewing needles will push through with light pressure.
